sentences.text: Yes, I came home.
And I was-- being the only boy in the house, I-- I was the one who-- who-- who ran errands.
You know, sometimes-- sometimes we didn't have enough food to put on the table.
Really, I can remember, literally, there wasn't bread enough.
I recall one Pesach, one Easter holiday-- you know, the main meals for us on Pesach were potatoes, matzot, and eggs.
Eggs.
There were Pesach night, the seder night would have been-- we would have been sitting down at Monday night.
The week before, Friday, we had-- we didn't have any potatoes, any matzot.
We didn't have, you mean, the basics.
We didn't have any eggs.
We had nothing, absolutely.
There wasn't any money to buy anything.
And being a proud family, my father was Hungarian born.
He was an officer in the Austro-Hungarian army, very, very proud man.
He would walk with his cylinder in the street, and everybody would bend, Mr.
Goldblatt, and would greet him.
So he wouldn't go and tell anybody that he needs something.
We were-- the motto was-- in Yiddish they would say, [SPEAKING YIDDISH].
Pinch your cheeks so they be red.
Nobody should know what is going on-- what's going on in the house.
Really, nobody knew.
My best friends, they were just kids in Sighet.
My closest friends, they never knew.
They wanted to go eat ice cream, I say my throat hurts.
If they wanted to go eat a [INAUDIBLE], I would say, no, I feel my stomach is not so well.
If they wanted to go to a cinema, I say my eyes hurt.
I would always have something that hurts to-- to get out of-- of going someplace with the-- with the boys.
They never-- after the war, I ask them, did they know-- they would com into our house, did they know-- did they have an idea how poor we were?
No, they said.
We never knew.
Everything was so clean and tidy and nice.
We never knew that.
So-- so really, it was a surprise for them.
Then I told them that I didn't go eat ice cream because I didn't have money, and I didn't go to the cinema because I didn't have money to pay for my ticket.
And we would never say to anybody, will you pay for me?
I mean, it would never enter my mind.
So that's-- they were so surprised.
They were surprised about that.
So where were we?
Oh, you were-- you'd been talking about how poor you were when you came back and being the-- the only boy-- So I was--
So that Pesach-- really, we didn't know, how are we going to make Pesach?
There isn't even-- even the-- the-- the [HEBREW].
Even the matzahs they aren't aren't there.
Potatoes-- this is the basic.
This is what you live on for eight days.
And then we had a-- we have an uncle in-- in a village not far from Sighet.
Sunday morning came in the uncle's son, and he would sell shingles.
He would sell shingles with what they covered the-- the-- the roofs, those kind of shingles, wood shingles.
And he brought in a-- a car-load of shingles.
And underneath, he brought a huge sack of sugar.
And he gave it to us.
And sugar was-- wasn't free to buy.
You couldn't-- even if you had the money, you couldn't buy it.
So we would-- my mother took the sugar and my father and they weighed it out in portions of kilos.
And we-- and I went to all-- I and my little sister, we went to all the Jewish houses, and we sold to everybody a kilo or two of sugar, and we made money.
And we bought everything that we needed for Pesach.
And whenever we meet our brothers and sisters, when we're together, we always remember the Pesach, the-- the happy Pesach that we had.
So if you want to call that a happy moment, yes, that Pesach was happy because the misery was so big.
Just two days earlier, we didn't know how we will make the Pesach-- the Seder-- the Seder night.
And now suddenly we had the matzot and the potatoes and whatever we needed for Pesach.
It was a happy, happy moment.
And then after Pesach, I think this was the last Pesach, actually, that we were free people.

Immediately, the-- the next Sunday after Pesach, it was the 9th of May, which I didn't remember, except that my-- now my sister sends me some pictures, and she writes on the 9th of May, we were deported.
So in the 9th of May, we were taken to-- to the ghetto.
And we were there for two weeks.
We were taken-- we-- we had to prepare-- they told us to prepare where to take the luggage, a cart.
You know, a cart with-- with oxen.
And all the neighbors, all the neighbors in that courtyard, we lived nine Jewish neighbors.
So all the neighbors used the same car, put it in on the-- put the valise, when there was no room, the rest we were carrying by hand.
They gave us notice on Thursday that on Sunday we have to leave for the ghetto.
Really, we didn't believe that this is going to happen.
Where are they going to take us?
You know, we believed that they are going to treat us like human beings, like-- like the neighbors we were.
But it wasn't to be.

Sunday morning, early they were starting hitting every soldier beside his rifle, these gendarmes had their sticks, and they were hitting with the sticks whatever would make noise just to put fear into us, and they rushed us out of the house and put everything, threw everything on that ox cart.
And they took us at the other end of the city.
It was about a mile and a half, I would say, that we walked a mile and a half.
They took us there.
Were there-- in that ghetto, we were-- the whole of Sighet, all the people of Sighet were in a few-- well, were-- they-- they fenced off a few streets.
And I remember the house of Elie Wiesel was the first house in that street to belong to the ghetto.
So they lived-- actually, they were not going out of their house.
They lived in the ghetto.
They were living in their house.
Only they added people to their-- to their-- you know, people were sleeping on the floor, I don't know.
You couldn't have so many-- so many beds.
We happened to have some relatives, some cousins who lived in those streets that were included in the ghetto.
So we went to live with them.
I mean, they brought you into that-- into that area where the ghetto was, and they said, take your luggage and go.
They didn't tell you where to go, you just went.
Now, everybody looked for somebody who would take him in.
We happened to have some cousins, so we lived with our cousins.
And we lived there-- we were-- we were-- we were the four sisters and father and mother.
Seven-- we were seven people.
And they were also about 10 people, my cousins with the children together.
The-- the husband and wife and the children.
And we were all huddled, and they had one bedroom and one kitchen.
That was their house.
And that's where we lived for like-- till the 13th or 14th of May.
And that's when they took us to the-- to the--
It wasn't really guarded.
It wasn't really guarded.
Was just fenced off.
And that's all.
You were-- you were afraid to go out.
I remember that we even went out-- went out over the-- over the fence.
Because it wasn't an electric fence.
It was just some wooden-- wooden planks, and that was all.
Some places had wire-- wire fences, it depends.
We-- we used to go out a few boys.
We would pick some apples from the trees.
We would-- we would play soccer outside, because in the ghetto there was no fields.
So it just-- outside of the ghetto, the house where we lived, there was a field, so we climbed over, and we played soccer.
And when we saw somebody, we-- we jumped back into the ghetto.
No, just the ghetto of Sighet.
No name.
Oh, the [INAUDIBLE] was the one that I remember.
The [INAUDIBLE].
The-- [INAUDIBLE] is the Street of Snakes, it was called.
That's-- the first house was the Wiesels's house, where Elie Wiesel lived.
And it was a long street.
And there was the [INAUDIBLE], the Gypsy Street.
And I can't recall so many.
There were quite a few streets that were in the ghetto, and you had to have 15,000 people.
Yes, we wore the yellow Star of David.
Yeah.
I remember they needed some people to help them rob the Jewish houses.
I mean, take out things from the Jewish houses.
So they took young-- some young boys, and I was one of them.
They took us out one day.
And we went through different houses.
And they-- they picked out anything that was portable they picked out for themselves.
There was one gendarme and a few friends of his, civilians.
Who-- who-- he wanted to benefit them.
And we picked out everything, and we loaded it onto a car.
And-- and they drove away with it.
So we worked for a whole day.
One day we went-- so I was out of the ghetto, officially escorted by-- by a gendarme.
And we were packing the valuables or whatever there was left in the Jewish houses that they couldn't take everything.
They didn't have the room, and they didn't have the time.
So whatever, I remember one of my friends, his violin was still on the-- on the bed, on the-- on the-- on the wall.
And we took it off, and one of the ladies took it, one of those women who came with the gendarmes, that they benefited from this.
And that's how they kept us busy.
Well, you can imagine when you see everything deserted, all the houses, all the streets, deserted.
No-- no one that you know, only the houses left.
Of course, very bad.
Very down.
But we were still young.
You know.
The minute we got a free moment, we went out to play soccer.
Food was very, very limited, because they-- they wouldn't supply you with anything.
I can't even imagine-- I-- I never cared about it so much.
You know?
That was my parents' job, my mother's job.
Not even my parents', not my father's-- my mother's job was to supply.
How they did it is beyond me.
I really don't know.
The things that we packed that would have lasted for two weeks, and we still had something-- some bread left in the wagons when we were on the way to Auschwitz.
I don't know how we did it.
I mean, it was very meager.
Meals-- real meals-- there weren't.
Bread, basically, eggs, and-- and that's it.
On the floor.
On the floor.
And when you say on the floor, you probably imagine that there were carpets on the floor.
On the floor, on the wooden floor.
If it was a wooden floor, some houses didn't have wooden floors, just-- just-- just from outside, like it's outside.
The plain floors.
The earth-- earthen floors.
And then on the 13th of May, they took us, the first transport.
The ghetto of Sighet was-- in three transports, they-- they-- they took them away-- took us away to Auschwitz.
I don't know.
There were-- to my estimation, there were living in Sighet, there were living 15,000 people, approximately.
But all the youngsters from 18 to 35 to 40, they were taken to the-- to the battalions of work that were digging ditches.
They were away or also they were not home.
I would say not because I know, only because I'm figuring out that our transport was like 1,200 to 1,500 people.
So each transport-- so there were 5,000 people probably or 6,000 people in a transport.
Our first-- we were in the first transport on the 13th of May.
We were taken by foot from that end of the city to another-- to somewhere in the middle of the town, to the big shul, to the-- to the big synagogue.
And we were there overnight.
Well, no.
There were 10 gendarmes, I remember that.
Because it occurred to me, 10 gendarmes and one German soldier, probably a sergeant.
The small pistol, like-- like even children today have bigger pistols when they go around-- that we see on television they use these huge pistols.
And-- and I was thinking that we were walking, like 1,200 or 1,500 people.
And we had all our cushions and-- and-- and then-- what do you call, our covers that we were sleeping in.
We had all that stuff with us.
If we were covering these people, nobody would see them.
And I was thinking, we are so many.
Why don't we go and-- and just-- these people, we can-- we can deal with them.
We can handle them.
Only 10 gendarmes.
And we are asked, you know, they tell us, why did you walk like this, like sheep?
And really, it's only-- I can only say one thing-- that the fear was so great.
They were rushing you all the time, beating you, pushing you, that you never had the moment of time to think that maybe we organize, and we attack these people.
We were not going.
Maybe we sit down on the floor and not going.
I can only quote-- there is a story about-- [INAUDIBLE] tells a story in one of his writings.
He says that they left-- 30,000 Jews left-- left Spain, and they were running towards Portugal.
And-- and he say that they arrived 3,000 out of the 30,000.
He says, I saw God Almighty fighting against the Jews.
He-- that's his declaration-- a very religious, pious man.
And he says, I saw God Almighty fighting against the Jews.
And this is the only thing.
If God puts the fear into you, you are so afraid that you cannot think for a moment.
But really, what would it have been, to kill 10, 11 people? 1,500 people against 11 people?
So they would have shot a few of us.
They were-- they didn't have automatic rifles like today.
You had to load every-- every bullet.
You have to load.
You shot one, you had to load.
Now, while he loaded, we could have killed him 1,000 times.
Why didn't we do it?
Because God fought against us.
God put fear into us.
We were so scared.
What can I tell you?
They were-- when you-- I'll tell you a little story.
In the shul, when you-- when we were sitting in the-- in the-- in the yard-- in the backyard of the shul, of the synagogue before they took you into the shul itself.
In the front of the shul, there was a little house, walk in.
An [INAUDIBLE] were set a few clerks and a few gendarmes.
And everybody-- all the valuables and all the money you would have to give them, and they would search you.
And if they found something on you, they would beat you very badly.
So we heard already the screamings outside.
So my father took what we had, a little money.
We went to the toilet, and we tear everything up, and we threw everything in the toilet, because we were afraid to show them that we have the money.
And my father walked in.
He was a very prominent looking man.
And he said in Hungarian, gentlemen, I am an [NON-ENGLISH].
I am a clerk, a county clerk.
I am a clerk of the-- of the state.
I was a clerk of the state all my life, and I know the law.
And if you tell me to give you everything we have, this is what we have.
And we put everything down what we had on the table except the money, because we were afraid to have money.
And we said, this is what we have, and this is my son, and this is what he has.
Nothing else.
And they were so impressed that they didn't even-- they didn't even check us.
And they gave-- and-- and they accepted and-- and let us into the shul.
But other people were checked, and pieces of clothes torn open.
And they would beat them and push them around.
And then we-- we were in the shul, sitting down on the floor, all of us, about 1,200 or 1,500 people.
And the next morning, they marched us to the train.
And we went on the train, 94 people in a cattle-- in a closed wagon.
There was only one little window.
Not a real window, really.
I was with my family.
Everybody was with their family.
No, Elie Wiesel came with the second, the next day.
Elie Wiesel came with the second or the third, or two days later.
Every day, the 13th, 14th, 15th, they were-- they were taking a transport.
He wasn't in my transport.
They started, it seems, from that side of the ghetto, and they moved forward, forward to this side of the ghetto.
He was on the other side of the ghetto.
In the ghetto, I've never even seen Elie Wiesel.
At least I don't even remember, because we were still afraid to walk so far.
They wouldn't allow us.
So we were staying close to-- everybody to his neighborhood where he lived.
In the train, we were 94 people sitting.
And we traveled for four and a half days to Auschwitz.
Now, after the first day, I don't recall which station.
The wagon being overloaded, our wagon started to burn.
So they drove us out, and they put us into another wagon, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a little bit-- a little bit bigger.
The wagon was a little bigger than the one before.
Before-- the one before was really a small wagon.
It was exactly the wagon-- the size of the wagon that I saw at the Holocaust Museum in Washington, where I was about four or five weeks ago to see.
It was exactly the size of the wagon, the same little window, which was with little planks.
You could-- you could look out a little and not see very much through it.
Because just-- it was a closed wagon.
No water, no bread, no food supplied for us.
Whatever we had from home, this little bread what they had, that's what we ate.
I remembered a woman in our wagon who said she has money, that if you give me when we arrive, I will give you money.
I have money with me.
And she was boasting about who was going to give her.
When we arrived, they took away all your clothes, so you couldn't even hide anything.
And they checked your body inside and out, and they cut your hair and took your glasses away and took your shoes away.
You were completely naked.
And--
No.
I remember one thing that was very typical, that each time the train stopped, somebody said, oh, there came an order.
They're turning us back.
There was a mistake.
They're taking us back home.
I mean, how did you know?
How did anyone-- the one who-- the one who-- who spread that rumor-- rumor, how did he know?
He was there with us.
But you didn't ask questions.
The hope was so big that they will take us back home, that this cannot happen to us.
This is not true.
You live like in a haze.
You were like-- like drugged.
I don't know, self-imposed, because you don't want to believe these things.
You don't want to believe that, that this is happening to you.
So each time the-- the train stopped, they would say, oh, they are taking us back.
And we went forward and forward and forward.
And even when we arrived to Auschwitz, they said, yeah, this is a working place.
We will-- we will on the fields here.
They always had something to say.
Nothing helped.
Did you arrive at Auschwitz?
Was it daytime or evening?
No, it was-- it was about-- I would say between 2 and 3 o'clock in the night.
And suddenly, you heard that big noise.
They were hitting the wagon all around, every wagon all at once.
They had people ready, inmates also.
Inmates and the SS people.
And dogs yelling, barking.
And they were hitting the-- the thing.
Out, out, [SPEAKING GERMAN].
They were-- as tired as you were, you were running.
You were grabbing whatever you-- you-- you saw first in your hand, near your hand.
And you were jumping off the train.
And we were forming already a line and [INAUDIBLE].
I remember, I was in the line.
They said, ladies separately and-- and men separately.
So I was in the first time with my father.
And an officer with a dog, with a huge dog.
And later we find out that his name was Mengele.
And he asked my father, how old are you?
And he said, 56.
He moved-- he made like this, a movement to one side.
And then he asked me how old.
I said, 17.
He made a movement this side.
And that's when I saw my father last.
And that's when I saw my family last.
My-- after we jumped out of the wagons, I didn't even see the girls.
I couldn't even see my mother, because they were a little bit further, another line, and they were not in the first line.
So I couldn't see them.
Not really, because the coming-- we were-- we were always hoping we would hear some good news.
Getting out of the train was so in a rush, so fast, so with-- with the noise, [SPEAKING GERMAN], this yelling and barking of the dogs, that you couldn't think of saying goodbye to anybody.
I never said goodbye to my father who was right near me.
We never-- didn't-- we were just waiting, what will happen.
And then suddenly, I found myself alone, and I wanted to go to the-- they told us that these are electric wires.
I wanted to go to the electric wires.
I mean, you wouldn't even reach the wire.
They would shoot you before you reach the wire, because they-- they had-- they had these towers with the SS people with rifles.
But I wanted to go to the wires.
What am I-- what, am I gonna be alone?
No mother, no father, nobody from the family?
And the boys from Sighet, I mean, the people, they made around me.
They circled me around and didn't let me out.
And they started talking to me.
It's not so bad.
It will be all right.
And one of these drivers who came to pick us up to take us into the camp-- this was right near the camp, in the camp, but not exactly where the barracks were, before the barracks.
So they were taking us with the truck.
They were loading us on the truck to take us to the barracks.
So the driver said in Hungarian, he spoke Hungarian, he said, don't worry.
You will meet your parents once a month.
And you will work, and you will be well fed.
You know, this is the way the Germans work.
All the time lying to you, making you believe so that you don't make-- make trouble, you don't start up any-- any trouble.
Then they took us into the barracks.
Before the barracks, they took us into the bath.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] A huge-- a huge room, a huge barrack kind of room, where they said, everybody undresses, take everything off.
Whatever you have, valuables, if you have valuables, this is the time to give it, because if we find it on you, you are dead.
So they would kill, if they found somebody-- somebody's stuck a gold coin in his rectum, they would look into the rectum.
They would check your teeth, write up how many gold teeth you have.
They would take your glasses.
They would take everything away.
I had some beautiful pair of shoes, and somebody offered me a loaf of bread.
They were my-- my brother's shoes.
They was-- he made them, handmade in Budapest.
And he sent them home.
So I-- I took the best pair of shoes when we go away from home.
And somebody offered me a loaf of bread.
I could have had a bite of bread then and there, but I said, I'm not going to give away. 10 minutes later, I left them there with the shoes.
You had to pile everything separately, the shoes separately, your valises separately, your glasses separately, everything separate.
And you undressed completely, and they checked you out.
And then they took you to a bath where they disinfected you.
Then you had to emerge yourself underneath.
You couldn't walk out.
A kind of a mikvah, like a little pool, the size of a mikvah.
And everyone in the line, you went in from this way.
You had to immerse yourself, and then you would go out the other-- the other side.
Then they gave you a pajama, which was [INAUDIBLE], which was a kind of a cap.
And the stripes, a shirt and a pair of pants, and a pair of Holland sandals, wooden sandals this go click, click, click.
Those kind of sandals they gave us.
And when we all-- we were all dressed, so-called dressed, and we were hoping that now they will put us somewhere to rest.
They took us into a barrack.
And then you heard the blockalteste, which was a huge guy, looking like some prize fighter, which he probably was, because they took the strongest ones and the ugliest ones and the frightening ones.
And he kept his short speech.
And he said, if one of you asks me for toilet paper, I kill him on the spot.
If one of you asks me more than one question, I kill him on the spot, and you will live eight in a-- in a bunk, and you don't move until I say move.
You don't go out of that bunk until I say go out.
And when I say go out, you do it immediately.
And if not, I kill you on the spot.
